system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

Full migration recipe in `references/standalone-content-fixure.md`. This applies to: navigation items, footer links, CTA hrefs, banner links, and any internal `href` or `to` field. Missing this causes dead links throughout the site post-deploy. See `references/standalone-content-fixure.md`. ### 0a. Duplicate JSON Keys After Content Extraction after loading, find all occurrences of the duplicate key at any nesting level with a recursive finder, merge the `items` arrays (deduplicating by `question`), then remove the nested duplicates. The packages exist and are published, but can't be installed reliably via `npm install` in any environment (local, Docker build, CI). The inline implementations are small — WhatsApp float, GA4 snippet, formatPrice — and should be baked directly into each app until the auth issue is resolved. ### 🔥 PAB Testimonials — Already Has Real Names (not initials) Contrary to initial browser-snapshot analysis, the PAB landing page testimonial data in `web/app/page.tsx` uses real names: - **Marcelo Ríos** — Fundador, Nexa Paraguay - **Dayah Benítez** — Diseñadora, Dayah Litworks - **Iván Giménez** — Chef, De Abasto a Casa The "M", "D", "I" visible in the browser snapshot were from the **stats counter** (value labels: 7+ years, 50+ authors, 5 countries), not testimonials. Common patterns: ```typescript // Pattern A: Array of objects (dayah-litworks) "navigation": [{ "label": "Inicio", "href": "/" }] // Pattern B: Object with navItems array (nexa-paraguay) "navigation": { "businessName": "...", "navItems": [{ "label": "Home", "href": "/" }], "ctaText": "Book a Call", "ctaHref": "https://..." } // Pattern C: Locale-keyed object in PAB (old multi-tenant format) "navigation": { "en": [...], "es": [...], "nl": [...] } ``` **Check first:** `typeof content.navigation` before writing the header component. If it's an object with a `navItems` key, use `c.navigation?.navItems || []`. If it's a raw array, use `c.navigation || []`. Getting this wrong causes `l.map is not a function` runtime error and crashes the page. See `references/nexa-multilocale-pattern.md` for the nexa-specific navigation structure. ### 0d. Content Resolver Pattern for Multi-Locale Sites Nexa (4 locales, 80KB/locale) stores data in a complex structure designed for the PAB engine: - Strings may be locale objects: `{"en": "...", "es": "...", "nl": "...", "de": "..."}` — must resolve to English - Images may be `{"$img": "programs.tierBase"}` — dot-path into images.json manifest, must resolve to `/images/programs/tier-base.webp` - Data paths differ: `home.programs.tiers` (not `home.programs.items`), `home.whyCountry.pillars` (not `home.whyCountry.items`) - Footer hrefs have `/s/en/nexa-paraguay` prefix — must batch-strip **Fix:** Pre-resolve all data at build time into a flat `content/resolved.json` (4KB instead of 80KB). Components import the resolved file, not the raw locale JSON. The resolver itself is a build-time tool, never imported at runtime. See `references/nexa-content-resolver-pattern.md` for the full implementation including the `rl()` locale resolution function, `IMAGE_PATHS` mapping, and the script to regenerate resolved.json. ### 0e. PNG Dedup for Docker Size Optimization The PAB's image pipeline generates both `.png` and `.webp` for every image. The `.png` files are never referenced by components — they're 10-20x larger than WebP. After extracting images to a standalone app: ```bash find public/images -name "*.png" -delete ``` This saves 100-150MB from the Docker image. Verify with `docker images {image}:prod --format "{{.Size}}"`. Do NOT delete `.png` brand images used as fallback for `` elements — check component usage with `grep -r "\.png" components/ app/` first. The Docker build output shows `ERROR: failed to build` with an exact file and line number. **That line number is real** — use `sed -n 'LINE-5,LINE+5p' path/to/file` to find the issue. Common errors: - **SyntaxError / "Expected '` tag nested inside SectionWrapper's `
`. Fix: change component's `
` to `
`. Portfolio-section.tsx is the common offender. * SECTION_MAP has alias collisions (e.g. `portfolio: 'portafolio'`). 4. **Next.js 15 `params` API change:** Dynamic route handlers MUST use `async function Page(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> })` and `await props.params`. The old synchronous `{ params: { slug } }` causes a TypeScript error about `Promise` type incompatibility. Always check the Next.js major-version-specific API docs when adding dynamic routes. The error message says: `Type '{ params: { slug: string; }; }' does not satisfy the constraint 'PageProps'. Types of property 'params' are incompatible. Type '{ slug: string; }' is missing the following properties from type 'Promise': then, catch, finally, [Symbol.toStringTag]`. Fix: both wrap the function with `async` and await `props.params`. - **Next.js 15 CSS import module resolution bug:** `Cannot find module or type declarations for side-effect import of './globals.css'` — This is a version-specific bug in Next.js 15.3.x with certain TypeScript configurations. Fix: **upgrade Next.js** (`npm install next@latest`) which installs 15.5+ and resolves the issue. The old fix (removing `import "./globals.css"` from layout.tsx) works but leaves Tailwind styles unloaded — the real fix is upgrading. Symptom: build succeeds but no CSS vars are applied in the browser. Also check for other Next-version-related issues like `next` binary not found in `npx next build` after copying node_modules from another project — do a clean `rm -rf node_modules && npm install`. ### 1. Image Serving — useFileSystemPublicRoutes: false Static files under `/public/sites/*` return 404. **NEVER** use `/sites/[tenant]/images/` paths — use `/images/[tenant]/` instead. Copy images to `web/public/images/[tenant]/covers/` for reliability. See `references/image-serving-rule.md`. ### 2. Section Variants — Wrong Variant = 404 Page Each section ID has EXACTLY the variants in `SECTION_CATALOG` (section-registry.ts). Setting an unregistered variant crashes the ENTIRE page. See `references/section-variant-registry.md` for the full table. ### 3. Build+Deploy Cycle — Every Change Goes Through Full Cycle ```bash cd web && npm run prebuild # Must succeed — generates tenant-data.ts + renderer-map.ts npm run build # Must succeed — Next.js build cd .. && docker build -f web/Dockerfile . -t paragu-ai:prod --no-cache docker service update --force --image paragu-ai:prod paragu-ai-builder_web sleep 20 curl -sI "https://paragu-ai.com/s/es/dayah-litworks" | head -3 docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep -E "Ready|composition|error|404" ``` Build must show NO ERRORS. Every change in `sites/[tenant]/` requires full cycle. Content-only edits (es.json) can skip Docker rebuild but still need prebuild+build. **Cache-busting trap:** `--no-cache` on Docker doesn't bust the Next.js `.next/` build cache. After editing shared components (section-builders.ts, site-renderer.tsx, portfolio-section.tsx), ALWAYS do `rm -rf web/.next && npm run build` to force a full rebuild. The old cache can silently serve stale compiled code. **Prebuild stale data trap:** `npm run prebuild` generates `web/lib/engine/generated/tenant-data.ts` which snapshots ALL tenant tokens at build time. Editing `sites/[tenant]/tokens.json` requires `npm run prebuild && npm run build` before Docker build. If you skip prebuild, the Docker image will contain old token values (like `--text-muted: #8890aa` instead of `#a0aac8`). After regeneration, verify with `grep \\\"textMuted\\\" web/lib/engine/generated/tenant-data.ts | grep \\\"dayah-litworks\\\" -A2 | head -5`. See `references/prebuild-verification.md` for the full stale-token debug recipe. **Old Docker containers surfacing after service update:** `docker service update --force` creates new containers but the old ones may still get HTTP traffic from the load balancer if they haven't fully shut down. The stale containers serve old compiled code causing phantom bugs. Fix: `docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=1 && sleep 15 && docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=3` to explicitly kill all old replicas. Verify with `docker ps --filter name=paragu-ai-builder --format \"{{.ID}} {{.Image}} {{.Status}}\"` — all must show the same SHA. **IMPORTANT:** after scaling down/up, wait until Docker Hub shows all containers with the same image SHA before declaring success. Run `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep -iE \"Ready|error|ReferenceError\"` to confirm no stale instances are still receiving traffic. If an old instance with a ReferenceError keeps appearing in logs, the load balancer is still routing to it — the scale-down didn't fully evict it. Force with `docker service update --force --image paragu-ai:prod paragu-ai-builder_web`. **Deploy trap — `docker service update --force` does NOT rebuild the image.** It only restarts containers with the EXISTING image tag. After any code change (components, section-builders, section-renderer, content JSON updates), you MUST run the full cycle: ```bash cd /tmp/pab-work/web && npm run prebuild && npm run build cd /tmp/pab-work && docker build -f web/Dockerfile . -t paragu-ai:prod docker service update --force paragu-ai-builder_web sleep 20 && curl -sI "https://paragu-ai.com/s/es/dayah-litworks" | head -1 ``` Content-only changes (es.json, en.json, home.json) DO need `npm run prebuild` to regenerate tenant-data, but can skip `npm run build` IF only JSON changed. Component changes ALWAYS need the full build cycle. **Stale container trap after update:** `docker service update --force` creates new containers but the OLD containers may STILL receive HTTP traffic from the load balancer if they haven't fully shut down. The stale containers serve OLD compiled code causing phantom bugs where the page shows old CSS vars (e.g. `--text-muted: #8890aa` instead of `#a0aac8`). Two fixes: 1. **Full reset:** `docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=1 && sleep 10 && docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=3` — this kills all old replicas explicitly. 2. **Verify:** `docker ps --filter name=paragu-ai-builder --format "{{.ID}} {{.Image}} {{.Status}}"` — all must show the SAME image SHA. Run `getComputedStyle(document.documentElement).getPropertyValue('--text-muted')` in browser console to confirm new token values took effect. See `references/deploy-stale-containers.md` for full debug recipe. **ReferenceError trap:** Adding `${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}` to a JSX template literal but forgetting to add `overlayColor` to (a) the TypeScript Props interface AND (b) the component's destructuring params causes a runtime `ReferenceError: overlayColor is not defined` that crashes the ENTIRE section via the error boundary. Fix: always add new props to the interface AND destructuring block simultaneously. Test by checking `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep "ReferenceError"` after deploy — if present, the fix is to patch both locations and redeploy. ## Subagent Page Imports Out of Sync (Expanded) When using `delegate_task` to create page files, the subagent may use different import paths or component names than actual files on disk. **This happened in BOTH dayah and elviajero builds** — the subagent created `.js` files instead of `.tsx`, imported `@/components/faq-section` instead of `@/components/faq`, and left `Header.js`/`Footer.js`/`WhatsAppFloat.js` alongside the `.tsx` versions. **Verification command after ANY subagent page creation:** ```bash # 1. List all component files that exist for f in components/*.tsx; do echo " $(basename $f .tsx)"; done echo "" # 2. List all component imports across pages for f in app/*/page.tsx app/page.tsx; do if [ -f "$f" ]; then echo "--- $f ---" grep 'from.*@/components' "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo " (none)" fi done # 3. Remove orphan .js files the subagent left behind rm -f components/Header.js components/Footer.js components/WhatsAppFloat.js 2>/dev/null # 4. Rebuild to catch any remaining dead imports npx next build 2>&1 | grep -E "error|Cannot find module" | head -5 ``` **Common mismatches to manually check:** - `@/components/whatsapp-float` vs non-existent path + kebab-case convention - `@/components/services-section` vs actual file name `services-section.tsx` or `services.tsx` - `@/components/faq-section` vs `faq.tsx` (component exports `FAQSection`) - `.js` files created alongside `.tsx` — Next.js ignores `.js` when `.tsx` exists but imports may hit either ## Client Questionnaire Gap Analysis Pattern When a client provides a detailed questionnaire response (like Dayah's 100% filled onboarding with service prices, testimonials, social links, and brand details), the gap-analysis pattern is: 1. **Save the raw questionnaire** as `docs/source-of-truth.md` — the canonical reference 2. **Generate an asset request** as `docs/asset-request.md` — 10-15 items across 3 priority tiers with Drive folder structure, prioritized by launch dependency 3. **Run gap analysis:** Compare every questionnaire section against `content/es.json` and `content/en.json`. Its content JSON (80KB/locale) uses a complex structure that the PAB engine normally resolves at runtime. For the standalone app, this was handled with two strategies: **Strategy 1: Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json` — 4KB, generated during build) The resolver (`lib/resolve.ts`) handles: 1. `{"$img": "programs.tierBase"}` → `/images/programs/tier-base.webp` (dot-path to file path) 2. Locale-aware objects `{"en": "...", "es": "..."}` → English value 3. Different data paths: `home.programs.tiers` (not `programs.items`), `home.whyCountry.pillars` (not `items`) 4. All nav/footer PAB paths batch-stripped **Strategy 2: Inline data for sub-pages** Pages like `/programs`, `/faq`, `/blog`, `/about` use hardcoded arrays instead of the content JSON, because the 80KB content structure has items nested at depths that don't match the component expectations. This is a pragmatic tradeoff — the JSON data is available for future content-management if the structure is normalized. **Key paths per page data source:** | Page | Data Source | Why | |------|-------------|-----| | Home | `content/resolved.json` | Resolver handles complex nesting + image refs | | /programs | Inline array (4 tiers) | Content has `programasPage.programs` as dict, not array | | /faq | Inline array (7 Q&A) | Content FAQ at `faqPage.full` with different structure | | /blog | Inline array (8 posts) | Blog posts in content are at `blog.index.posts` but empty | | /about | Inline HTML + array (6 cards) | About page has `differentiators` not `description` | **Reference:** `references/nexa-content-resolver-pattern.md` for the full resolver implementation, inline IMAGE_PATHS mapping, and locale resolution pipeline. ### Nexa Blog + Sub-Page Data Strategy The Nexa content JSON (80KB/locale) has blog posts, FAQ items, and program data nested at depths that DON'T match the page components. Two strategies emerged: **Strategy A — Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json`): For the HOME PAGE, pre-resolve all data at build time. The resolver handles `$img` refs, locale objects, and different data paths. Components import the resolved JSON (4KB) instead of the raw content (80KB). **Strategy B — Inline data for sub-pages** (programs, faq, blog, about): These pages use hardcoded arrays because the 80KB content structure has items in paths like `programasPage.programs` (dict, not array of items), FAQ items in `faqPage.full` (non-standard format), and blog posts in `blog.index.posts` (empty array in the JSON — actual posts are MDX files in a `blog/` directory). **When to use which:** ```typescript // Strategy A — content matches component expectations // Use resolved.json when you can flatten the data paths // Strategy B — content structure doesn't match // Use inline arrays when: // - Content JSON has empty arrays for expected fields // - Data is stored in objects instead of arrays // - Items use non-standard field names // - Content exists in external files (MDX, separate JSON) ``` **Blog post page with Next.js 15 async params (no MDX):** ```tsx // app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx const posts = [...]; // inline array export default async function BlogPost(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const { slug } = await props.params // MUST await params in Next.js 15 const post = posts.find(p => p.slug === slug) if (!post) notFound() // ... render post } ``` **Blog cards linking to [slug]:** ```tsx // app/blog/page.tsx — cards must be not

{p.title}

Coming Soon

) } ``` This replaces 7+ individual `app/{page}/page.tsx` files with ONE dynamic route. Every undefined route returns 200 with a branded "Coming Soon" message instead of a 404. The catch-all route MUST be the LAST route file alphabetically — Next.js matches catch-all routes last. #### Navigating Without Full Context — Zero-Context Agent Rescue When resuming a session or starting fresh without context compression working (no auxiliary LLM configured): 1. Building a full product store requires creating product data or pulling from bundles JSON. The 62KB content is primarily marketing copy, navigation, blog posts (10 posts), and legal text (age compliance, returns, privacy). **Blog posts** ARE available as individual `.mdx` files at `/tmp/pab-work/sites/fun4me/blog/es/` with full article content (not just metadata in the JSON). This causes push failures or unacceptable repo bloat. **Always clean before pushing:** ```bash # 1. Before git commit — remove build cache rm -rf .next git add -A git commit -m "..." # 2. For repos that already have .next/ in git history — force push without it # .gitignore MUST include: echo -e "node_modules/\n.next/\n*.log\n.env\n.env.local" > .gitignore rm -rf .next git add .gitignore . git commit -m "clean: remove build cache" git push -u origin main --force ``` **Symptom of committed .next cache:** `! [remote rejected] main -> main (pre-receive hook declined)` — the remote has size limits. If this happens, the ONLY fix is `--force` after removing the cache files. ### 2x Footer Link Nav → Reduce on Catch-All Pages When a site has a `

This produces console noise like: ``` GET https://site.com/prensa?_rsc=... 404 (Not Found) ``` This is harmless (the catch-all returns 200) but noisy. Two fixes: 1. Use `` instead of `` for catch-all-bound footer links (no prefetch) 2. Or live with it — it's prefetch noise, not real errors ### Deploy Pitfall — Service Not Reachable After `docker stack deploy` This is a surprisingly common failure mode. The sequence: deploy looks successful (no errors), service shows 2/2 replicas, overlay test returns 200 — but external HTTPS returns 000. Debug checklist: 1. **Check Traefik labels are applied:** `docker service inspect {stack_name}_web --format '{{json .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}' | python3 -m json.tool` — if only `com.docker.stack.namespace` is present, the labels were NOT applied. This happens when `docker stack deploy` receives a compose file with `build:` instead of `image:` — it silently drops `deploy.labels` too. 2. **Check SSL cert status:** `docker service logs traefik_traefik --tail 20 2>&1 | grep -i '{domain}'` — if you see `rateLimited`, Let's Encrypt has banned the domain for ~1h due to too many failed authorization attempts. Cloudflare proxy mode + LetsEncrypt HTTP-01 = the challenge hits Cloudflare's IP, not the origin, causing repeated failures. 3. **Stack name collision:** If a client was deployed with different stack names (e.g. `alejandro_villayamor` then `alejandro`), the OLD Traefik service discovery still has the old stack's container info. `docker stack rm {old_stack}` resolves it. Always remove ALL prior stacks before redeploying a migrated client. 4. **Certificate verification lag:** Even after Traefik issues a cert, it can take 30-60 seconds for Cloudflare's edge to cache the new cert. The initial requests hit 526 while propagation completes. **Fix for LetsEncrypt rate limit (alejandro-villamayor case):** Switch Cloudflare SSL/TLS to "Flexible" (Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Flexible) or set the DNS record to grey cloud (DNS only) until the cert is issued, then switch back. ## Nexa Architecture — Reference for Multi-Locale Standalone Sites ``` nexa.paragu-ai.com ├── content/ │ ├── en.json (79KB — source of truth for builds) │ ├── es.json (83KB) │ ├── nl.json (82KB) │ ├── de.json (82KB) │ └── resolved.json (4KB — pre-resolved EN data for components) ├── lib/ │ └── resolve.ts (build-time content resolver — NOT used at runtime) ├── app/ │ ├── page.tsx (6 sections: hero + programs + pillars + process + testimonials + CTA) │ ├── programs/page.tsx (4 tiers with pricing, inline data) │ ├── about/page.tsx (story + 6 differentiators) │ ├── faq/page.tsx (7 accordion Q&A, inline) │ ├── blog/page.tsx (8 post cards, inline) │ ├── contact/page.tsx (Calendly + WhatsApp buttons) │ ├── [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all for 7+ stub pages) │ └── not-found.tsx ├── components/ (header.tsx, footer.tsx, whatsapp-float.tsx) └── public/images/ (187 WebP images, 0 PNGs) ``` **Critical:** The resolver is a BUILD-TIME tool only. Running it at import time in components means every request loads and resolves 80KB of JSON. The resolved output is written to `content/resolved.json` and components import THAT file instead. The `lib/resolve.ts` file exists for regeneration, not runtime use. **Cleanup reference:** `references/standalone-asset-audit.md` for the full asset audit process (identifying PNG duplicates, orphan SVGs, unused brand files). ### Common Standalone App Pitfalls 1. **`delegate_task` subagent fails with 401** — OpenRouter/provider auth errors prevent parallel execution. When subagent calls fail, fall back to sequential execution directly in the main session. The subagent system uses a different model (claude-sonnet-4 by default) which may be misconfigured or hitting rate limits. If `delegate_task` returns `Error code: 401 - {'error': {'message': 'User not found.'}}`, debug with `hermes config get models` or work around with terminal + `python3 << 'PYEOF' ... PYEOF` for batch operations. 2. **Auto-generated lead sites are TWO versions behind** — The PAB generator produces sites with an older template missing: `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` in Dockerfile, `traefik.docker.network=agent-net` label, healthchecks, non-root user, security headers, 404/error pages, robots/sitemap. Always apply `references/lead-site-deployment-checklist.md` from the standalone-ecommerce-store skill when deploying lead-generated sites. 1a. **Navigation structure varies by tenant — NEVER assume array shape.** Different tenants store nav in completely different structures. Getting this wrong causes `l.map is not a function` runtime error: ```typescript // Pattern A: Array (dayah-litworks) "navigation": [{ "label": "Inicio", "href": "/" }] // Pattern B: Object with navItems (nexa-paraguay) "navigation": { "businessName": "...", "navItems": [{ "label": "Home", "href": "/s/en/nexa-paraguay" }] } ``` **Fix:** Check with `typeof content.navigation`. For object with `navItems`, use `c.navigation?.navItems || []`. For array, use `c.navigation || []`. 1b. **Nav hrefs may contain PAB paths (`/s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas`).** After fixing the structure (step 1a), the href values themselves are wrong — they reference old PAB routes. Batch-replace in the header component: ```typescript const href = (n.href || n.path || "").replace("/s/en/nexa-paraguay", "") || "/" ``` Without this, clicking a nav link goes to `/sobre` (correct) but `prefetchNextRouter` on the `` prefetches the munged path (e.g. `/object Object`) causing `[object Object] 404` errors in console. The same `.replace()` must be applied in BOTH desktop and mobile nav loops. 1c. **PAB nav href prefixed with `/` + `.replace()` creates `//s/en/...` paths.** If your code does `"/" + href` BEFORE the replace, the link becomes `"//s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas"` which Next.js renders as `[object Object]`. The order must be: extract href → strip PAB prefix → prepend `/` if empty. 1d. **`@/` import alias may fail in freshly scaffolded apps.** The `@/*` path alias in tsconfig.json may not resolve correctly if `tsconfig.json` has `baseUrl` misconfigured or the file was copied from another project without regeneration. ## WHAT IS A STROOPWAFEL? (Blog/About Section)

### Title: ¿Qué es un Stroopwafel?

**Copy**:
El stroopwafel es una galleta tradicional holandesa que se inventó en la ciudad de Gouda, Países Bajos, alrededor del año 1784. Un panadero llamado Gerard Kamphuisen creó la primera versión usando restos de masa de pan y endulzándolos con un almíbar de especias.

**¿Cómo se hace?**
1. Se prepara una masa con harina, manteca, azúcar, huevo, levadura y canela
2. Se presiona en una plancha caliente (como una waflera, pero más fina)
3. Se abre la galleta en dos mitades mientras está caliente
4. Se rellena con un almíbar de caramelo con canela (llamado "stroop")
5. Se cierra y ¡listo!

**El secreto holandés**: Poné tu stroopwafel sobre tu taza de café caliente. El vapor calienta el caramelo y lo hace suave y derretido. Así lo toman los holandeses desde hace más de 200 años.

**Fun fact**: Los Países Bajos producen más de 22 millones de stroopwafels por semana. Ahora, por primera vez, los podés disfrutar frescos en Paraguay.", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

# Dayah LitWorks — AI Agent Guide

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://dayah.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/dayah-litworks
- **Docker service:** dayah-litworks_web (2 replicas)
- **VPS:** 72.61.44.159
- **Traefik:** Host(`dayah.paragu-ai.com`)

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → dayah-litworks_web:3000

## Pages
13 pages: Home, Servicios, Premades, Portafolio, Blog (5 posts), Nosotros, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Términos

## Design System
Dark theme (navy/plum background, #0a0a14/var(--background)). Primary: #94abd6 (muted blue). Secondary: #d43d5e (rose). Fonts: Playfair Display (headings), Inter (body). Voice: warm, creative, Spanish.

## Content
Content lives in `content/es.json` (and other locales when applicable).
All text, services, products, FAQ items are editable there.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
npm run build
docker build -t dayah-litworks:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml dayah-litworks
```

## Critical Patterns
- All content in content/es.json and content/en.json
- No database — fully static
- WhatsApp is the primary CTA
- Dark theme with gradient accents

## Client Onboarding
See `docs/client-questionnaire.md` for the full onboarding questionnaire.
See `docs/brand-guide.md` for brand identity details.

{"content": "# Stroopwafel Huis — AI Agent Guide

## Quick Links
- **Live:** https://stroopwafelhuis.paragu-ai.com
- **Repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/stroopwafel-huis
- **Business plan repo:** github.com/Ai-Whisperers/stroopwafel-huis (same repo, business docs in root)

## Architecture
Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → stroopwafel-huis_web:3000

## Concept
First Dutch-themed specialty coffee shop in Paraguay. Fresh handmade stroopwafels + specialty coffee. Founders: Bram & Rach.

## Pages
11 pages: Home, Menu, Historia, Ubicación, Catering, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Términos

## Design System
Warm Dutch theme. Primary: #FF6B00 (Dutch Orange). Secondary: #C8860A (Caramel). Accent: #003082 (Delft Blue). Background: #FFF5E6 (Warm Cream). Fonts: Playfair Display (headings), Inter (body). Voice: warm, Dutch-inspired, Spanish.

## Content
Content lives in `src/content/es.json`. All menu items, brand copy, FAQ, pages are editable there.

## Build & Deploy
```bash
cd /root/stroopwafel-huis
npm run build
docker build -t stroopwafel-huis:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml stroopwafel-huis
```

## Critical Patterns
- All content driven from src/content/es.json — no database
- WhatsApp is the primary CTA
- Weekly flavor rotation (Crumbl model) is the killer feature
- Dutch orange + warm cream color palette throughout
- All SVG icons inline (lucide-react v1.14 dropped Instagram/Facebook)

## Business Docs (from original repo)
- `menu/menu-complete.md` — Full menu with prices
- `branding/brand-identity.md` — Brand identity, colors, logo
- `website/design/website-full-spec.md` — Full website specification
- `business-plan/business-plan.md` — Complete business plan
- `recipes/` — All recipes
- `operations/` — Daily operations guides

Adding New Business Type (Most Common Task) When adding a new business type (e.g., `dentist`, `mechanic`): **Required Files:** ``` src/ ├── registry/[type].type.json # Section config, features ├── tokens/[type].tokens.json # Colors, fonts ├── content/[type].content.json # Spanish copy templates └── schemas/[type].schema.json # Validation schema ``` **Reuses:** - ✅ Existing section components from `web/components/sections/` - ✅ Base tokens from `src/tokens/base.tokens.json` - ✅ No new React components needed (usually) **Example (5-minute add):** ```json // src/registry/dentist.type.json { "id": "dentist", "nameEs": "Consultorio Dental", "tokens": "dentist", "sections": ["hero", "services", "team", "testimonials", "contact"], "features": { "booking": true, "emergency": true } } ``` ### 2. Adding New Section Component When adding a new reusable section: **File:** `web/components/sections/[name]-section.tsx` **Pattern:** ```typescript interface [Name]SectionProps { title: string items: Array<{...}> // Theme automatically applied via CSS variables } export function [Name]Section({ title, items }: [Name]SectionProps) { return (

{title}

) } ``` **Registration:** Add to `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx` ### 3. Test by checking `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep "ReferenceError"` after deploy — if present, the fix is to patch both locations and redeploy. ## Subagent Page Imports Out of Sync (Expanded) When using `delegate_task` to create page files, the subagent may use different import paths or component names than actual files on disk. **This happened in BOTH dayah and elviajero builds** — the subagent created `.js` files instead of `.tsx`, imported `@/components/faq-section` instead of `@/components/faq`, and left `Header.js`/`Footer.js`/`WhatsAppFloat.js` alongside the `.tsx` versions. **Verification command after ANY subagent page creation:** ```bash # 1. List all component imports across pages for f in app/*/page.tsx app/page.tsx; do if [ -f "$f" ]; then echo "--- $f ---" grep 'from.*@/components' "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo " (none)" fi done # 3. Rebuild to catch any remaining dead imports npx next build 2>&1 | grep -E "error|Cannot find module" | head -5 ``` **Common mismatches to manually check:** - `@/components/whatsapp-float` vs non-existent path + kebab-case convention - `@/components/services-section` vs actual file name `services-section.tsx` or `services.tsx` - `@/components/faq-section` vs `faq.tsx` (component exports `FAQSection`) - `.js` files created alongside `.tsx` — Next.js ignores `.js` when `.tsx` exists but imports may hit either ## Client Questionnaire Gap Analysis Pattern When a client provides a detailed questionnaire response (like Dayah's 100% filled onboarding with service prices, testimonials, social links, and brand details), the gap-analysis pattern is: 1. **Save the raw questionnaire** as `docs/source-of-truth.md` — the canonical reference 2. **Generate an asset request** as `docs/asset-request.md` — 10-15 items across 3 priority tiers with Drive folder structure, prioritized by launch dependency 3. **Run gap analysis:** Compare every questionnaire section against `content/es.json` and `content/en.json`. Its content JSON (80KB/locale) uses a complex structure that the PAB engine normally resolves at runtime. For the standalone app, this was handled with two strategies: **Strategy 1: Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json` — 4KB, generated during build) The resolver (`lib/resolve.ts`) handles: 1. `{"$img": "programs.tierBase"}` → `/images/programs/tier-base.webp` (dot-path to file path) 2. Locale-aware objects `{"en": "...", "es": "..."}` → English value 3. Different data paths: `home.programs.tiers` (not `programs.items`), `home.whyCountry.pillars` (not `items`) 4. All nav/footer PAB paths batch-stripped **Strategy 2: Inline data for sub-pages** Pages like `/programs`, `/faq`, `/blog`, `/about` use hardcoded arrays instead of the content JSON, because the 80KB content structure has items nested at depths that don't match the component expectations. This is a pragmatic tradeoff — the JSON data is available for future content-management if the structure is normalized. **Key paths per page data source:** | Page | Data Source | Why | |------|-------------|-----| | Home | `content/resolved.json` | Resolver handles complex nesting + image refs | | /programs | Inline array (4 tiers) | Content has `programasPage.programs` as dict, not array | | /faq | Inline array (7 Q&A) | Content FAQ at `faqPage.full` with different structure | | /blog | Inline array (8 posts) | Blog posts in content are at `blog.index.posts` but empty | | /about | Inline HTML + array (6 cards) | About page has `differentiators` not `description` | **Reference:** `references/nexa-content-resolver-pattern.md` for the full resolver implementation, inline IMAGE_PATHS mapping, and locale resolution pipeline. ### Nexa Blog + Sub-Page Data Strategy The Nexa content JSON (80KB/locale) has blog posts, FAQ items, and program data nested at depths that DON'T match the page components. Two strategies emerged: **Strategy A — Pre-resolved JSON** (`content/resolved.json`): For the HOME PAGE, pre-resolve all data at build time. The resolver handles `$img` refs, locale objects, and different data paths. Components import the resolved JSON (4KB) instead of the raw content (80KB). **Strategy B — Inline data for sub-pages** (programs, faq, blog, about): These pages use hardcoded arrays because the 80KB content structure has items in paths like `programasPage.programs` (dict, not array of items), FAQ items in `faqPage.full` (non-standard format), and blog posts in `blog.index.posts` (empty array in the JSON — actual posts are MDX files in a `blog/` directory). **When to use which:** ```typescript // Strategy A — content matches component expectations // Use resolved.json when you can flatten the data paths // Strategy B — content structure doesn't match // Use inline arrays when: // - Content JSON has empty arrays for expected fields // - Data is stored in objects instead of arrays // - Items use non-standard field names // - Content exists in external files (MDX, separate JSON) ``` **Blog post page with Next.js 15 async params (no MDX):** ```tsx // app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx const posts = [...]; // inline array export default async function BlogPost(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const { slug } = await props.params // MUST await params in Next.js 15 const post = posts.find(p => p.slug === slug) if (!post) notFound() // ... render post } ``` **Blog cards linking to [slug]:** ```tsx // app/blog/page.tsx — cards must be not

{p.title}

Coming Soon

) } ``` This replaces 7+ individual `app/{page}/page.tsx` files with ONE dynamic route. Every undefined route returns 200 with a branded "Coming Soon" message instead of a 404. For repos that already have .next/ in git history — force push without it # .gitignore MUST include: echo -e "node_modules/\n.next/\n*.log\n.env\n.env.local" > .gitignore rm -rf .next git add .gitignore . git commit -m "clean: remove build cache" git push -u origin main --force ``` **Symptom of committed .next cache:** `! [remote rejected] main -> main (pre-receive hook declined)` — the remote has size limits. If this happens, the ONLY fix is `--force` after removing the cache files. ### 2x Footer Link Nav → Reduce on Catch-All Pages When a site has a `

This produces console noise like: ``` GET https://site.com/prensa?_rsc=... 404 (Not Found) ``` This is harmless (the catch-all returns 200) but noisy. Two fixes: 1. Or live with it — it's prefetch noise, not real errors ### Deploy Pitfall — Service Not Reachable After `docker stack deploy` This is a surprisingly common failure mode. The sequence: deploy looks successful (no errors), service shows 2/2 replicas, overlay test returns 200 — but external HTTPS returns 000. Debug checklist: 1. **Check Traefik labels are applied:** `docker service inspect {stack_name}_web --format '{{json .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}' | python3 -m json.tool` — if only `com.docker.stack.namespace` is present, the labels were NOT applied. This happens when `docker stack deploy` receives a compose file with `build:` instead of `image:` — it silently drops `deploy.labels` too. 2. **Check SSL cert status:** `docker service logs traefik_traefik --tail 20 2>&1 | grep -i '{domain}'` — if you see `rateLimited`, Let's Encrypt has banned the domain for ~1h due to too many failed authorization attempts. Cloudflare proxy mode + LetsEncrypt HTTP-01 = the challenge hits Cloudflare's IP, not the origin, causing repeated failures. 3. **Stack name collision:** If a client was deployed with different stack names (e.g. `alejandro_villayamor` then `alejandro`), the OLD Traefik service discovery still has the old stack's container info. `docker stack rm {old_stack}` resolves it. Always remove ALL prior stacks before redeploying a migrated client. 4. **Certificate verification lag:** Even after Traefik issues a cert, it can take 30-60 seconds for Cloudflare's edge to cache the new cert. The initial requests hit 526 while propagation completes. **Fix for LetsEncrypt rate limit (alejandro-villamayor case):** Switch Cloudflare SSL/TLS to "Flexible" (Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Flexible) or set the DNS record to grey cloud (DNS only) until the cert is issued, then switch back. ## Nexa Architecture — Reference for Multi-Locale Standalone Sites ``` nexa.paragu-ai.com ├── content/ │ ├── en.json (79KB — source of truth for builds) │ ├── es.json (83KB) │ ├── nl.json (82KB) │ ├── de.json (82KB) │ └── resolved.json (4KB — pre-resolved EN data for components) ├── lib/ │ └── resolve.ts (build-time content resolver — NOT used at runtime) ├── app/ │ ├── page.tsx (6 sections: hero + programs + pillars + process + testimonials + CTA) │ ├── programs/page.tsx (4 tiers with pricing, inline data) │ ├── about/page.tsx (story + 6 differentiators) │ ├── faq/page.tsx (7 accordion Q&A, inline) │ ├── blog/page.tsx (8 post cards, inline) │ ├── contact/page.tsx (Calendly + WhatsApp buttons) │ ├── [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all for 7+ stub pages) │ └── not-found.tsx ├── components/ (header.tsx, footer.tsx, whatsapp-float.tsx) └── public/images/ (187 WebP images, 0 PNGs) ``` **Critical:** The resolver is a BUILD-TIME tool only. Running it at import time in components means every request loads and resolves 80KB of JSON. The resolved output is written to `content/resolved.json` and components import THAT file instead. ```bash
